quoteFirst: He says: “On the 12th day of June, in the year 1467, the noble duke Philip of Burgundy was seized with a grievous malady, which continued unabated until Monday, the 15th, when he rendered his soul to God, between nine and ten o’clock at night. When he perceived, on the preceding day, that he was growing worse, he sent for his son, the count de Charolais, then at Ghent, who hastened to him with all speed; and on his arrival,
quoteLast: of the Monday, at the duke’s palace in Bruges, he went instantly to the chamber where the duke lay sick in bed, but found him speechless. He cast himself on his knees at the bedside, and, with many tears, begged his blessing, and that, if he had ever done anything to offend him, he would pardon him.
title: The historians' history of the world in twenty-five volumes, volume 11
author: Williams, Henry Smith, 1863-1943 [Editor]

quoteFirst: Leave La Cloche and march to Esquebecq, entraining there at 8.30 a.m. for Audruicq. Embus at Audruicq for Listergaux, arriving
quoteLast: . Received orders that the 7th Camerons are to break up and proceed to the 6th Battalion. Lieut.-Col. F.
title: The History of the 7th Battalion Queen's Own Cameron Highlanders
author: Sandilands, James Walter; MacLeod, Norman, 1872-1960

quoteFirst: When the little party got back to camp, carrying a number of poles for the beginning of the hut, they found Captain Spark preparing to take an observation, as it was
quoteLast: .  He asked Mr. Carr to assist him.
title: Bob the Castaway; Or, The Wreck of the Eagle
author: Webster, Frank V.

quoteLast: we left the plain, and almost at once our ponies began to stand up like pet dogs begging for cake, their hind legs supporting their weight while front hoofs pawed for foothold above on the stairlike, rocky trail. An Albanian held each of us tightly by elbow or knee, ready to save us from squashy death if the pony lost its balance, and as the little animals strained, clambered, gathered their feet together for desperate leaps, a sudden long high wail broke forth ahead. The two gendarmes were singing.
title: Peaks of Shala
author: Lane, Rose Wilder, 1886-1968

quoteLast: when I started, and the sun was shining cheerfully enough overhead but it was hell on earth. A short distance out I passed one of the English cars coming in and the driver shouted to me to have my gas mask ready. I confess I had a feeling of fear.
title: An American Crusader at Verdun
author: Rice, Philip Sidney, 1878-1927

quoteLast: we encamped close to Chinhut, and Hope Grant took special care that day to see the piquets were well placed, for the rebels were in great numbers, and we were surrounded by ravines and wooded enclosures. It was thought by some that he was unnecessarily anxious and careful, for he rode several times over the ground; but the next morning proved how right he was to leave nothing to chance.
title: Forty-one years in India: from subaltern to commander-in-chief
author: Roberts, Frederick Sleigh Roberts, Earl, 1832-1914

quoteFirst: When morning came I was only too thankful to get up, and when my friend arrived on her bicycle
quoteLast: , and asked me how I had slept in the strange house, I was forced to confess that my night had been much troubled by dreams about an old friend, of whom she had never heard, by-the-by.
title: Seen and Unseen
author: Bates, E. Katherine (Emily Katherine)

quoteFirst: The only sound of life which reached us was the call of the Serracúra (Gallinula Cayennensis), a kind of wild-fowl; all else was so still that the voices of boatmen could be plainly heard from canoes passing a mile or two distant from us. The sun soon gains great power on the water, but with it the sea-breeze increases in strength, moderating the heat which would otherwise be almost insupportable. We reached the end of the Goajará
quoteLast: , and then entered the narrower channel of the Mojú. Up this we travelled, partly rowing and partly sailing between the same unbroken walls of forest, until the morning of the 28th.
title: The Naturalist on the River Amazons
author: Bates, Henry Walter, 1825-1892

quoteFirst: Atticus resumed: "When sheep are fed continually in the same locality distinction must be made in the times of feeding them according to the seasons: thus in summer they are driven out[121] to pasture at day break because then the dewy grass is more appetizing than at midday, when it is dry. At sunrise they are driven to water, to make them more lickerish on their return.
quoteLast: and during the heat of the day they are permitted to lie in the shade of rocks or under broad spreading trees until the fresher evening air invites them to feed again until sunset.[122] A sheep should always graze with the sun behind it, because its head is very sensitive to heat.
title: Roman Farm Management: The Treatises of Cato and Varro
author: Cato, Marcus Porcius, 235 BCE-150 BCE; Varro, Marcus Terentius, 117 BCE-28 BCE; Harrison, Fairfax, 1869-1938 [Translator]

quoteLast: on the 14th, the 3rd Corps continued the advance, and after some considerable fighting secured, by 7 p.m., the line Bleu--east of Bailleul--Neuve Église.
author: French, John Denton Pinkstone, Earl of Ypres, 1852-1925
